questions.q: What is a B+ tree and how does it differ from a B tree?
options: B+ trees are slower than B trees
A B+ tree stores all values in leaf nodes and maintains leaf-to-leaf links for efficient range scans; B trees store values at all nodes
B+ trees cannot be used for indexing
There is no practical difference between them
questions.explanation: B+ trees segregate keys (internal nodes) from values (leaf nodes) with leaf sibling links enabling sequential scans. This sacrifices point lookup slightly but excels at ranges. B trees store values everywhere, better for point queries but worse for scanning.

questions.q: What is an LSM (Log-Structured Merge) tree?
options: A type of data compression algorithm
A write-optimized tree structure that buffers writes in memory (memtable), flushes to sorted immutable files (SSTables), and periodically merges SSTables to maintain read performance
A tree used only in NoSQL databases
A method for preventing tree rebalancing
questions.explanation: LSM trees optimize writes by deferring merging (compaction) to background threads. Writes go to in-memory memtable, then immutable SSTables. Reads check memtable then SSTables in reverse age order. Compaction merges SSTables, eliminating deletes and duplicates.

questions.q: Why are LSM trees better for write-heavy workloads?
options: LSM trees are not better for writes
LSM trees turn random writes into sequential writes to log and memtable, maximizing throughput; B+ trees require random disk updates during rebalancing
LSM trees compress data automatically
LSM trees use less memory than B+ trees
questions.explanation: LSM sequential writes to log+memtable are fast; B+ tree rebalancing causes random disk I/O. On modern hardware, sequential I/O vastly outperforms random I/O. For write-heavy systems (databases, caches), LSM is superior.

questions.q: What is a memtable in an LSM tree?
options: A temporary backup of the disk data
An in-memory data structure (usually a balanced tree or skip list) that buffers recent writes before flushing to disk as an immutable SSTable
A file containing metadata about SSTables
A cache for frequently accessed values
questions.explanation: The memtable is an in-memory buffer for writes. When full (size threshold), it's flushed to disk as an immutable SSTable. Multiple SSTables accumulate; compaction merges them. Memtable is critical for write latency and throughput.

questions.q: What is an SSTable and what does immutability enable?
options: SSTable is a backup format
An SSTable (Sorted String Table) is an immutable sorted file on disk; immutability enables safe concurrent reads, efficient compression, and background compaction without locking
SSTables are used only by NoSQL databases
Immutability makes SSTables impossible to modify
questions.explanation: SSTables are immutable: no locks needed for concurrent access. Compaction safely merges SSTables in background threads without disrupting reads. New writes accumulate in new SSTables; old SSTables are discarded when compacted. This isolation is fundamental to LSM design.

questions.q: What is compaction in LSM trees and why is it necessary?
options: Compaction is an optional optimization that can be skipped
Compaction merges multiple SSTables into fewer, larger ones to eliminate duplicates, deletes, and maintain read performance; without it, reads degrade as SSTable count grows
Compaction is only used for data backup
Compaction deletes all old data permanently
questions.explanation: As memtables flush, SSTable count grows. Reading requires checking all SSTables, degrading lookup performance. Compaction merges SSTables: reads many, writes one, eliminating redundancy. Trade-off: write amplification (rewriting data) for read efficiency.

questions.q: What is write amplification and how do LSM trees suffer from it?
options: Write amplification does not affect LSM trees
A phenomenon where compaction causes the same data to be rewritten multiple times; a single logical write may be rewritten 10x or more during compaction cycles
Write amplification only affects B+ trees
Write amplification is when writes are split across multiple files
questions.explanation: LSM compaction rewrites data repeatedly. If data is compacted L levels and each level triggers merging, the same byte is written many times. High write amplification reduces SSD lifespan and wastes I/O. Tuning compaction strategy (leveled vs tiered) helps.

questions.q: How do B+ trees handle range queries efficiently?
options: They scan all leaf nodes sequentially
They use the B+ tree index structure to locate the start of the range, then follow leaf-node sibling pointers sequentially without jumping through the tree
They convert range queries to point queries
B+ trees do not handle range queries efficiently
questions.explanation: B+ tree leaf nodes are linked; a range query uses the tree to find the start leaf, then follows sibling pointers. This avoids re-traversing the tree for each row. LSM must check multiple SSTables, making range scans slower.

questions.q: What is leveled compaction in LSM trees?
options: A backup strategy for data safety
A compaction strategy where SSTables are organized in levels; each level is 10x the size of the previous; compaction keeps each level non-overlapping
A method to encrypt data in SSTables
Leveled compaction is not used in real databases
questions.explanation: Leveled compaction: L0 (new SSTables), L1 (10x size), L2 (100x size), etc. Each level is non-overlapping (no key overlap). Lookup checks at most one SSTable per level (log search depth). Trade-off: lower write amplification but higher compaction cost.

questions.q: Why do modern databases like RocksDB and Cassandra choose LSM trees over B+ trees?
options: LSM trees are always better than B+ trees
LSM trees maximize write throughput and sequential I/O, critical for high-volume writes and modern SSD hardware where sequential I/O dominates
B+ trees are obsolete
LSM trees use less disk space
questions.explanation: LSM trees turn random writes into sequential I/O, matching SSD behavior. B+ tree rebalancing causes random I/O, underutilizing modern hardware. For high-throughput systems (streaming, analytics), LSM is the natural choice. Traditional SQL databases often keep B+ for their different workload.
